title: Investigation Site Details
description: What investigators find at a crime scene, ritual site, or abandoned building — the specific details that accumulate into a picture.

entries.result: Every clock in the building stopped at the same moment.
A meal was in preparation. The ingredients are laid out. Nothing has been touched since.
A single chair placed in the center of an otherwise empty room, facing a blank wall.
Scratch marks on the inside of a closet door at a consistent height, low, as if made over a long period.
The personal effects of several different people gathered in one room and sorted into groups.
A journal with all pages torn out except the last, which reads: 'It worked.'
Photographs taken down from walls and stacked face-down, neatly.
A circle has been cleared on the floor — furniture moved to the walls, rugs rolled aside, the floor beneath scoured.
Several candles burned down to the last, their holders arranged at equal distances.
A window broken from the inside, the glass outside the building.
Books pulled from shelves and arranged in a specific order that doesn't match alphabetical, subject, or size.
A bed with the sheets removed and folded precisely on the floor beside it.
An address written on a piece of paper, no other context.
A set of personal effects laid out as if at the foot of a grave — items from a specific person's life, arranged with care.
A name repeated on every available surface, written in different handwriting.
Marks on the wall at regular intervals from floor to chest height, as if someone was tracking time.
All the mirrors covered with cloth — methodically, using matching material.
A locked box that contains another locked box.
An item that belongs to someone known to be alive and elsewhere.
All the food in the building moved into a single room and stacked.
Soil tracked in from outside in a consistent path — to a specific room, stopping.
A photograph of this room, taken recently, that shows something that is not there now.
A series of numbers written in sequence on the underside of a table — dates, or coordinates, or neither.
An unfinished letter — mid-sentence — addressed to the investigators by name.
A handprint pressed into the ceiling of a low-clearance room.
Multiple sets of handwriting in a single notebook that progressively converge into one hand.
A room where every surface has been washed carefully except one corner.
All the locks in the building changed and only one key present — placed in an obvious location.
A child's drawing on the floor of a room that otherwise shows no sign of children.
Several personal items — a watch, a ring, a wallet — that do not belong together and show no connection.
A door to a room nailed shut from the inside.
A map with one location circled and all routes to it marked.
Evidence that the building was used for extended habitation — recently, by someone who was meticulous about leaving no trace.
Several voices captured on a recording device found on the floor, speaking in sequence and then all at once.
A list of names — some crossed out, some not — with no indication of what the list is for.
Personal correspondence hidden inside a container that had no apparent purpose for concealment.
A section of floor recently repaired and the materials used don't match the building's age.
A complete calendar for a month years prior with specific dates marked in a pattern that takes time to recognize as intentional.
A chair pulled close to a wall with ear-height markings on the wall, as if someone was listening.
The same phrase written in six different languages on six walls.
A medication bottle empty and carefully wiped down, the label removed.
A collection of the same object — forty-three of the same item, gathered from what appear to be many sources.
A set of keys hanging on the wall, each labeled, for locations the investigators have not been to yet.
A locked room that, when finally opened, contains only a chair and a recently extinguished candle.
A document shredded and then reassembled by someone else, the tape holding it together fresh.
Something buried shallowly under the floor — not a body, not a container, something harder to categorize.
A series of dated notes leading to a final note dated yesterday.
Evidence of two distinct inhabitants at two different periods, their presence overlapping by a day.
A drawing of the building's floorplan with one room that doesn't correspond to anything the investigators can find.
A window sealed with material from inside, from the inside, that would have required someone to be inside when it was done — and sealed completely.
All the personal photographs removed and replaced with photographs of the same subjects taken from outside, through windows.
A working telephone with the last number dialed displayed — the number is the investigators' own contact number.
A room arranged to be completely observed from a single point — the furniture positioned so that everything in the room is visible from one chair.
A hidden room accessed through a concealed door that opens to a space too small to be useful but containing a single object.
The occupant's shoes left by the door — both pairs, including spares — while the occupant is gone.
A layer of ash in a bathtub that is not from the building's fireplace.
A series of objects arranged in a precise pattern that, once identified as a pattern, is clearly interrupted — one object moved or missing.
A diary with entries that describe, in present tense, events that happened decades ago.
A single window in the building with its view obstructed by a sheet from outside — placed by someone who didn't want those inside looking out, not someone who didn't want those outside looking in.
All the building's light sources gathered and placed together in one room.
A door that opens into a wall — the wall built after the door, not before.
Water glasses filled and placed at regular intervals through the building.
A fire in the fireplace that has been burning for longer than any normal fire should with available fuel.
A name carved into the underside of every windowsill, each in a different hand.
A collection of hair — from several different people — braided together and hung from a central point in a room.
Newspapers covering every surface, all the same edition, all the same date.
A complete inventory of the building's contents, accurate, written in a hand that matches no known occupant.
A series of photographs taken in sequence that document something happening in this building — something that involved the person who took them in a way that makes the final photograph significant.
A room that appears untouched until the investigators look up.
A letter of apology — specific, detailed, addressed to someone who, as far as the investigators can tell, was not wronged.
Evidence that multiple people slept in this building and left simultaneously — the impression precise enough to suggest they left in a hurry at the same moment.
A bookshelf with all books removed, a single book left face-down, open to a specific page.
A recording of this room, from an unknown device, showing what was here before the investigators arrived — including something that left before they entered.
A series of holes in the wall at consistent intervals, at eye height, each covered carefully and then uncovered again.
An item belonging to someone the investigators know is currently missing.
The same sentence, in the same handwriting, on twelve scraps of paper distributed through the building in places that took effort to reach.
A set of instructions for performing a task — complete, careful, practical — with the final step missing.
Blood in a pattern that is not consistent with injury — spread with intent, dried carefully.
A list of previous addresses for a person, each corresponding to a location associated with something bad, ending with this address.
Everything in the building labeled in a system the investigators don't immediately recognize but that, once deciphered, describes a highly specific organizational logic.
An account book with transactions recorded in a standard format except that the currency used doesn't correspond to anything the investigators recognize.
A section of the ceiling above a specific point in the room worn smooth — as if touched, repeatedly, over a long period.
Two identical items in the building — same age, same wear, same origin — that should exist in only one copy.
A space that appears to have been lived in entirely from a reclining position — all furniture, all systems, all necessities arranged for someone who did not stand up.
An answering machine full of messages — all from different callers, none knowing each other — all describing the same event in different terms.
A set of research notes that arrive at a conclusion and then attempt, in growing desperation, to find an alternative conclusion.
A room that has been systematically emptied of everything soft — cushions, bedding, curtains — and replaced with hard, reflective surfaces.
A collection of newspaper clippings, all about unrelated events, connected by a thread the investigators can see but cannot name.
A set of carvings in a beam — not decorative, sequential, depicting something in panels like a story.
Three locks on the inside of a single door, each from a different era of locksmithing, each added later than the last.
A window with a perfect view of the location where something significant happened, positioned deliberately.
A series of names in a guest register — all fictional, all using names from a specific source — and one name that is real.
Evidence of a fire that was started and then extinguished before it could take hold, repeatedly, in the same location.
A children's book, old, with one page altered — a figure added to an illustration in ink that matches the book's age.
A functional room entirely encased in a second room built around it — the original room intact, the new construction filled with years of accumulation.
Something growing in the basement — not mold, not roots, something organized.
A map of the building that shows rooms the investigators have already been through differently — walls in the wrong places, a room marked where there is currently only a solid wall.
The building's guestbook, complaint register, or visitor log with every entry describing the same experience in different words.
Everything the investigators expected to find — perfectly placed, perfectly preserved, perfectly explaining everything — and one detail that makes the perfect explanation impossible.
The investigators' own names, their current addresses, and accurate descriptions of what they were doing before they arrived here, written in a document dated six months ago.
